evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Advise people with impetigo, and their parents or carers if appropriate, about good hygiene measures to reduce the spread of impetigo to other areas of the body and to other people. For a short explanation of why the committee made the recommendation, see the rationale section on advice to reduce the spread of impetigo . Full details of the evidence and the committee's discussion are in evidence review .

heading_path: Advice to reduce the spread of impetigo

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider hydrogen peroxide 1% cream for people with localised non-bullous impetigo who are not systemically unwell or at high risk of complications (see recommendations on choice of antimicrobial ). Although other topical antiseptics are available for treating superficial skin infections, no evidence was found for using them to treat impetigo.

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider hydrogen peroxide 1% cream for people with localised non-bullous impetigo who are not systemically unwell or at high risk of complications (see recommendations on choice of antimicrobial ). Although other topical antiseptics are available for treating superficial skin infections, no evidence was found for using them to treat impetigo.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: If hydrogen peroxide 1% cream is unsuitable, offer a short course of a topical antibiotic for people with localised non-bullous impetigo who are not systemically unwell or at high risk of complications (see recommendations on choice of antimicrobial ).

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: If hydrogen peroxide 1% cream is unsuitable, offer a short course of a topical antibiotic for people with localised non-bullous impetigo who are not systemically unwell or at high risk of complications (see recommendations on choice of antimicrobial ).

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Offer a short course of a topical or oral antibiotic for people with widespread non‑bullous impetigo who are not systemically unwell or at high risk of complications (see recommendations on choice of antimicrobial ). Take into account: that topical and oral antibiotics are both effective at treating impetigo the preferences of the person and, if appropriate, their parents or carers, including the practicalities of administration (particularly to large areas) and possible adverse effects previous use of topical antibiotics because antimicrobial resistance can develop rapidly with extended or repeated use.

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Offer a short course of a topical or oral antibiotic for people with widespread non‑bullous impetigo who are not systemically unwell or at high risk of complications (see recommendations on choice of antimicrobial ). evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Do not offer combination treatment with a topical and oral antibiotic to treat impetigo. For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations, see the rationale section on initial treatment . Full details of the evidence and the committee's discussion are in evidence review .

For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations, see the rationale section on initial treatment . Full details of the evidence and the committee's discussion are in evidence review .

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Advise people with impetigo, and their parents or carers if appropriate, to seek medical help if symptoms worsen rapidly or significantly at any time, or have not improved after completing a course of treatment.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: When reassessing people with impetigo, take account of: other possible diagnoses, such as herpes simplex any symptoms or signs suggesting a more serious illness or condition, such as cellulitis previous antibiotic use, which may have led to resistant bacteria.

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: When reassessing people with impetigo, take account of: other possible diagnoses, such as herpes simplex any symptoms or signs suggesting a more serious illness or condition, such as cellulitis previous antibiotic use, which may have led to resistant bacteria.

evidence_statements.setting: Community, primary care, and hospital reassessment of impetigo.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE antimicrobial and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: For people with impetigo that is worsening or has not improved after treatment with hydrogen peroxide 1% cream, offer: a short course of a topical antibiotic if the impetigo remains localised or a short course of a topical or oral antibiotic if the impetigo has become widespread (see the recommendation on widespread non-bullous impetigo ).

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For people with impetigo that is worsening or has not improved after treatment with hydrogen peroxide 1% cream, offer: a short course of a topical antibiotic if the impetigo remains localised or a short course of a topical or oral antibiotic if the impetigo has become widespread (see the recommendation on widespread non-bullous impetigo ).

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: For people with impetigo that is worsening or has not improved after completing a course of topical antibiotics: offer a short course of an oral antibiotic (see the recommendations on choice of antimicrobial ) and consider sending a skin swab for microbiological testing.

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For people with impetigo that is worsening or has not improved after completing a course of topical antibiotics: offer a short course of an oral antibiotic (see the recommendations on choice of antimicrobial ) and consider sending a skin swab for microbiological testing.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: For people with impetigo that is worsening or has not improved after completing a course of oral antibiotics, consider sending a skin swab for microbiological testing.

evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE antimicrobial and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: For people with impetigo that recurs frequently: send a skin swab for microbiological testing and consider taking a nasal swab and starting treatment for decolonisation .

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For people with impetigo that recurs frequently: send a skin swab for microbiological testing and consider taking a nasal swab and starting treatment for decolonisation .

evidence_statements.population: Adults, children, and young people with impetigo, including reassessment after treatment or recurrent disease.
evidence_statements.setting: Community, primary care, and hospital reassessment of impetigo.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE antimicrobial and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: If a skin swab has been sent for microbiological testing: review the choice of antibiotic when results are available and change the antibiotic according to results if symptoms are not improving, using a narrow-spectrum antibiotic if possible. For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations, see the rationale section on reassessment and further treatment for impetigo . Full details of the evidence and the committee's discussion are in evidence review .

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: If a skin swab has been sent for microbiological testing: review the choice of antibiotic when results are available and change the antibiotic according to results if symptoms are not improving, using a narrow-spectrum antibiotic if possible. evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider referral or seeking specialist advice for people with impetigo if they: have bullous impetigo, particularly in babies (aged 1 year and under) have impetigo that recurs frequently are systemically unwell are at high risk of complications. For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations, see the rationale section on referral and seeking specialist advice for impetigo . Full details of the evidence and the committee's discussion are in evidence review .

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: When prescribing an antimicrobial for impetigo, take account of local antimicrobial resistance data when available and follow: Table 1 for adults aged 18 years and over Table 2 for children and young people under 18 years. Table 1 Antimicrobials for adults aged 18 years and over Treatment Antimicrobial, dosage and course length Topical antiseptic Hydrogen peroxide 1% : Apply two or three times a day for 5 days First-choice topical antibiotic if hydrogen peroxide is unsuitable (for example, if impetigo is around eyes) or ineffective Fusidic acid 2% : Apply three times a day for 5 days Alternative topical antibiotic if fusidic acid resistance is suspected or confirmed Mupirocin 2% : Apply three times a day for 5 days First-choice oral antibiotic Flucloxacillin : 500 mg four times a day for 5 days Alternative oral antibiotic for penicillin allergy or if flucloxacillin is unsuitable (for peo

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Impetigo characterised by thin-walled vesicles or pustules that rupture quickly, forming a golden-brown crust.

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Impetigo characterised by thin-walled vesicles or pustules that rupture quickly, forming a golden-brown crust.

evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE antimicrobial and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Impetigo characterised by the presence of fluid-filled vesicles and blisters often with a diameter of over 1 cm that rupture, leaving a thin, flat, yellow-brown crust.

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Impetigo characterised by the presence of fluid-filled vesicles and blisters often with a diameter of over 1 cm that rupture, leaving a thin, flat, yellow-brown crust.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Use of topical treatments (antiseptic body wash, nasal ointment or a combination of both) and personal hygiene measures to remove the bacteria causing the infection from the body.
